trajectory.description: Observation: Starting the experiment. Analysis: We need to train an RL agent on 10 Minigrid environments. To establish a baseline and measure iteration time, I will train a separate PPO model for each environment for a very small number of timesteps (10,000). I will use a custom observation wrapper to flatten the image and direction into a 1D array, allowing the use of SB3's MlpPolicy. Decision: Write wrapper.py, train.py, and predict.py, install minigrid, run the training script, and submit for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The baseline with 10k timesteps and a flattened MLP policy achieved 0.0 success rate, which is expected due to the very short training time and suboptimal architecture. Analysis: Minigrid environments benefit from CNNs to process the 7x7 grid effectively. Also, 10k timesteps is far too few. Decision: I will implement a custom CNN feature extractor for SB3, use SubprocVecEnv with 16 workers to speed up training, and increase the training timesteps to 500,000 per environment. I will also increase the entropy coefficient to 0.05 to encourage exploration.

trajectory.description: Observation: The baseline model achieved 0.398 success rate. Environments requiring memory and exploration (DoorKey, KeyCorridor, LockedRoom, etc.) failed completely. Analysis: Minigrid is partially observable (7x7 view), making exploration and memory very hard. By applying `FullyObsWrapper`, we can make the environments fully observable, removing the need for memory (RNN) and drastically simplifying exploration. I will also increase the training timesteps for the harder environments to 2M, and use a more robust CNN architecture with stride=2 to handle varying grid sizes efficiently. Decision: Update model.py with the new CNN, update train.py and predict.py to use FullyObsWrapper, increase timesteps, and run training.

trajectory.description: Observation: The success rate dropped to 0.044 with FullyObsWrapper. Analysis: FullyObsWrapper changes the observation to allocentric (full grid), which is much harder for the CNN to process than the egocentric view, as the agent has to learn its own position and map it to egocentric actions. To solve memory-dependent tasks (KeyCorridor, LockedRoom) while keeping the egocentric view, we need a recurrent policy. Decision: Revert to egocentric view, implement a proper Embedding-based CNN for the categorical image channels, and use RecurrentPPO from sb3-contrib to handle memory. I will train for 11M timesteps total.

trajectory.description: Observation: The installation of `sb3-contrib` failed because network access is disabled during the training phase. Analysis: I cannot use `RecurrentPPO`. I must revert to standard `PPO` from `stable-baselines3`. To compensate for the lack of recurrence, I will significantly increase the training timesteps (to 15M total) and rely on the CNN's ability to extract spatial features. The increased training time should allow the agent to solve most environments through extensive exploration. Decision: Update `train.py` and `predict.py` to use `PPO`, increase timesteps, and run training.

trajectory.description: Observation: The standard PPO model achieved 0.54 success rate. Environments requiring memory and complex interactions (LockedRoom, UnlockPickup, MultiRoom) failed. Analysis: The agent lacks memory of past observations and its own inventory (carrying status is not in the default egocentric image). Decision: I will implement frame stacking (k=3) to provide short-term memory and explicitly add the agent's 'carrying' status to the observation space. I will also increase the training timesteps for the harder environments to 4M and use a higher entropy coefficient (0.05) to encourage exploration.

trajectory.description: Observation: The frame stacking and complex wrapper in iter 10 significantly slowed down training (took 3.66 hours) and reduced the success rate to 0.318. We only have 1.67 hours left in the time budget. Analysis: I need to revert to the simpler, faster architecture from iter 9 (which scored 0.54) to ensure the training finishes within the remaining time budget. I will use the embedding-based CNN without frame stacking and allocate a total of 10.5M timesteps across the environments, which should take about 1.2-1.4 hours. Decision: Rewrite wrapper.py, model.py, train.py, and predict.py to the iter 9 setup, run training, and submit for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The training script failed with a RuntimeError: 'Tensors must have same number of dimensions: got 2 and 3' when concatenating `cnn_out` and `dir_emb`. Analysis: The `direction` observation from SB3 has shape `(batch_size, 1)`, which makes the embedding output `(batch_size, 1, 16)`. We need to flatten or squeeze the direction tensor before passing it to the embedding layer so it outputs `(batch_size, 16)`. Decision: Fix `model.py` by adding `.view(-1)` to the direction tensor. I will also slightly reduce the total timesteps to 9M to ensure the training finishes comfortably within the remaining 1.67 hours of the time budget.

Observation: The training script failed with a RuntimeError because `direction` was one-hot encoded by SB3 to shape `(batch_size, 4)`, and `view(-1)` flattened it to `(batch_size * 4,)`, causing a shape mismatch in `torch.cat`. Analysis: Since SB3 automatically one-hot encodes `Discrete` spaces in `Dict` observations, `observations['direction']` is already a float tensor of shape `(batch_size, 4)`. We don't need an embedding layer for it; we can just concatenate it directly with the CNN output. Decision: Update `model.py` to remove the direction embedding and concatenate the one-hot direction directly. I will also set the total timesteps to 12M, which should take about 1.5 hours, fitting perfectly within the remaining 1.67 hours of the time budget.

trajectory.description: Observation: The training script failed with a RuntimeError because `mat1 and mat2 shapes cannot be multiplied (16x1348 and 3140x256)`. Analysis: The issue is that SB3 automatically transposes image observations from `(H, W, C)` to `(C, H, W)` if the `dtype` is `np.uint8` and it has 3 channels. This caused our `(7, 7, 3)` image to become `(3, 7, 7)`, which messed up the CNN input shape and resulted in a flattened size of 1344 instead of 3136. Decision: I will fix this by changing the `dtype` of the image observation to `np.int32` in `wrapper.py` (so SB3 doesn't treat it as an image to transpose) and also add a safety transpose check in `model.py`. I will set the total timesteps to 10.5M to ensure it finishes safely within the remaining 1.66 hours.

trajectory.description: Observation: The training script failed with a RuntimeError because `direction` was 3D, causing a shape mismatch in `torch.cat`. Analysis: SB3's Dict observation space preprocessing can sometimes result in unexpected shapes depending on how it handles Discrete spaces and batching. To make the model completely robust, I will add explicit shape checks and reshaping logic for both `image` and `direction` in `model.py`. This will ensure `direction` is always `(batch_size, 4)` and `image` is always `(batch_size, 7, 7, 3)` before processing. Decision: Update `model.py` with robust shape handling, keep the total timesteps at 10.5M to ensure it finishes within the remaining 1.66 hours, run training, and submit for evaluation.
